prompt: Tell me one thing I'm probably wrong about — not a hypothetical me, just a stranger writing to you. Pick something.
response:
You’re probably wrong if you believe multitasking makes you more productive.

Why: what people call multitasking is usually rapid task-switching. Each switch carries a cognitive cost — extra time to reorient, more mistakes, and poorer memory for details. Lab studies and practical workplace research consistently find that people working on two tasks at once complete them more slowly and with lower quality than when they focus on one at a time.

How to test it on yourself: time how long it takes to finish a 20–30 minute focused task without interruptions, then try the same task while switching between messages, tabs, or another task every few minutes. You’ll likely take longer and make more errors in the latter.

If this sounds like you: try single-task blocks (25–90 minutes), batch similar small tasks, and turn off notifications. That usually yields more work done, better results, and less exhaustion than “doing everything at once.”
